subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the Lewis dot structure of the CO molecule.
question_html: Write the Lewis dot structure of the CO molecule.
solution_text: Step 1. Count the total number of valence electrons of carbon and oxygen atoms. The outer (valence) shell configurations of carbon and oxygen atoms are: 2s 2 2p 2 and 2s 2 2p 4 , respectively. The valence electrons available are 4 + 6 =10. Step 2. The skeletal structure of CO is written as: CO Step 3. Draw a single bond (one shared electron pair) between C and O and complete the octet on O, the remaining two electrons are the lone pair on C. This does not complete the octet on carbon and hence we have to resort to multiple bonding (in this case a triple bond) between C and O atoms. This satisfies the octet rule condition for both atoms.
solution_html:
Step 1. Count the total number of valence electrons of carbon and oxygen atoms. The outer (valence) shell configurations of carbon and oxygen atoms are:
2s2 2p2 and 2s2 2p4, respectively. The valence electrons available are 4 + 6 =10.
Step 2. The skeletal structure of CO is written as: CO
Step 3. Draw a single bond (one shared electron pair) between C and O and complete the octet on O, the remaining two electrons are the lone pair on C.

This does not complete the octet on carbon and hence we have to resort to multiple bonding (in this case a triple bond) between C and O atoms. This satisfies the octet rule condition for both atoms.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the Lewis structure of the nitrite ion, {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} .
question_html: Write the Lewis structure of the nitrite ion, {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex}.
solution_text: Step 1. Count the total number of valence electrons of the nitrogen atom, the oxygen atoms and the additional one negative charge (equal to one electron). N(2s 2 2p 3 ), O (2s 2 2p 4 ) 5 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6) +1 = 18 electrons Step 2. The skeletal structure of {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} is written as: O N O Step 3. Draw a single bond (one shared electron pair) between the nitrogen and each of the oxygen atoms completing the octets on oxygen atoms. This, however, does not complete the octet on nitrogen if the remaining two electrons constitute lone pair on it. Hence we have to resort to multiple bonding between nitrogen and one of the oxygen atoms (in this case a double bond). This leads to the following Lewis dot structures.
solution_html:
Step 1. Count the total number of valence electrons of the nitrogen atom, the oxygen atoms and the additional one negative charge (equal to one electron).
N(2s2 2p3), O (2s2 2p4)
5 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6) +1 = 18 electrons
Step 2. The skeletal structure of {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} is written as: O N O
Step 3. Draw a single bond (one shared electron pair) between the nitrogen and each of the oxygen atoms completing the octets on oxygen atoms. This, however, does not complete the octet on nitrogen if the remaining two electrons constitute lone pair on it.

Hence we have to resort to multiple bonding between nitrogen and one of the oxygen atoms (in this case a double bond). This leads to the following Lewis dot structures.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain the structure of {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} ion in terms of resonance.
question_html: Explain the structure of {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} ion in terms of resonance.
solution_text: The single Lewis structure based on the presence of two single bonds and one double bond between carbon and oxygen atoms is inadequate to represent the molecule accurately as it represents unequal bonds. According to the experimental findings, all carbon to oxygen bonds in {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} are equivalent. Therefore the carbonate ion is best described as a resonance hybrid of the canonical forms I, II, and III shown below. Resonance in {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} , I, II and III represent the three canonical forms.
solution_html:
The single Lewis structure based on the presence of two single bonds and one double bond between carbon and oxygen atoms is inadequate to represent the molecule accurately as it represents unequal bonds. According to the experimental findings, all carbon to oxygen bonds in {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} are equivalent. Therefore the carbonate ion is best described as a resonance hybrid of the canonical forms I, II, and III shown below.

Resonance in {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}, I, II and III represent the three canonical forms.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain the structure of the CO 2 molecule.
question_html: Explain the structure of the CO2 molecule.
solution_text: The experimentally determined carbon to oxygen bond length in CO 2 is 115 pm. The lengths of a normal carbon to oxygen double bond (C = O) and carbon to oxygen triple bond (C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O) are 121 pm and 110 pm respectively. The carbon-oxygen bond lengths in CO 2 (115 pm) lie between the values for C = O and C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O. Obviously, a single Lewis structure cannot depict this position and it becomes necessary to write more than one Lewis structures and to consider that the structure of CO 2 is best described as a hybrid of the canonical or resonance forms I, II and III.
solution_html: The experimentally determined carbon to oxygen bond length in CO2 is 115 pm. The lengths of a normal carbon to oxygen double bond (C = O) and carbon to oxygen triple bond (C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O) are 121 pm and 110 pm respectively. The carbon-oxygen bond lengths in CO2 (115 pm) lie between the values for C = O and C{tex} \equiv {/tex}O. Obviously, a single Lewis structure cannot depict this position and it becomes necessary to write more than one Lewis structures and to consider that the structure of CO2 is best described as a hybrid of the canonical or resonance forms I, II and III.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain the formation of chemical bonds.
question_html: Explain the formation of chemical bonds.
solution_text: According to Walther Kissel and Gilbert N. Lewis atoms combine together in order to complete their respective octets so as to acquire the stable inert gas configuration. This can occur in two ways by transfer of one or more electrons from one atom to other or by sharing of electrons between two or more atoms.According to a combination of atoms, there is a different type of bonds are formed 'strong bonds' like a metallic bond, covalent bond , and ionic bond etc. and 'weak bonds' like dipole-dipole attraction etc.
solution_html: According to Walther Kissel and Gilbert N. Lewis atoms combine together in order to complete their respective octets so as to acquire the stable inert gas configuration. This can occur in two ways by transfer of one or more electrons from one atom to other or by sharing of electrons between two or more atoms.According to a combination of atoms, there is a different type of bonds are formed 'strong bonds' like a metallic bond, covalent bond, and ionic bond etc. and 'weak bonds' like dipole-dipole attraction etc.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define Octet rule. Write its significance and limitations.
question_html: Define Octet rule. Write its significance and limitations.
solution_text: Octet rule: Atoms of elements combine with each other in order to complete their respective octets so as to acquire the stable gas configuration, Significance: It helps to explain why different atoms combine with each other to form ionic compounds or covalent compounds. Limitations of Octet rule: According to Octet rule, atoms take part in chemical combination to achieve the configuration of nearest noble gas elements. However, some of the noble gas elements like Xenon have formed compounds with fluorine and oxygen. For example: XeF 2 , XeF 4 etc. Therefore, the validity of the octet rule has been challenged. The octet rule is not applicable for all the non-metals. It is not applicable after Silicon because they can expand their octet. The octet rule is not applicable for those molecules which contain odd no. of electrons.
solution_html:
Octet rule: Atoms of elements combine with each other in order to complete their respective octets so as to acquire the stable gas configuration,
Significance: It helps to explain why different atoms combine with each other to form ionic compounds or covalent compounds.
Limitations of Octet rule:

  1. According to Octet rule, atoms take part in chemical combination to achieve the configuration of nearest noble gas elements. However, some of the noble gas elements like Xenon have formed compounds with fluorine and oxygen. For example: XeF2, XeF4 etc. Therefore, the validity of the octet rule has been challenged.
  2. The octet rule is not applicable for all the non-metals. It is not applicable after Silicon because they can expand their octet.
  3. The octet rule is not applicable for those molecules which contain odd no. of electrons.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the favorable factors for the formation of the ionic bond.
question_html: Write the favorable factors for the formation of the ionic bond.
solution_text: The favorable factors for the formation of the ionic bond are: (i) Low ionization enthalpy of metal atoms (ii) High electron gain enthalpy of non-metal atoms. (iii) High Lattice enthalpy of the compound formed.
solution_html:
The favorable factors for the formation of the ionic bond are:

(i) Low ionization enthalpy of metal atoms
(ii) High electron gain enthalpy of non-metal atoms.
(iii) High Lattice enthalpy of the compound formed.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How do you express the bond strength in terms of bond order?
question_html: How do you express the bond strength in terms of bond order?
solution_text: Bond strength is directly proportional to the bond order. Greater the bond order more is the bond strength.
solution_html: Bond strength is directly proportional to the bond order. Greater the bond order more is the bond strength.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define the bond Length.
question_html: Define the bond Length.
solution_text: Bond Length: It is the average distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms in a molecule. Bond Lengths are measured by microwave spectroscopic methods in gases phase and by X-ray diffraction in the solid phase.
solution_html: Bond Length: It is the average distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms in a molecule. Bond Lengths are measured by microwave spectroscopic methods in gases phase and by X-ray diffraction in the solid phase.

question_html: Explain the important aspect of resonance with reference to the {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}ion.
solution_text: Resonance in, CO 3 2- I, II and III represent the three canonical forms. In these structures, the position of nuclei is the same. All the three forms have the almost equal energy The same number of paired and unpaired electrons they differ only in their position.
solution_html:
Resonance in, CO32- I, II and III represent the three canonical forms.

  1. In these structures, the position of nuclei is the same.
  2. All the three forms have the almost equal energy
  3. The same number of paired and unpaired electrons they differ only in their position.

question_text: H 3 PO 3 can be represented by structures 1 and 2 shown below. Can these two structures be taken as the canonical forms of the resonance hybrid representing H 3 PO 3 ? If not, give reasons for the same.
question_html: H3PO3 can be represented by structures 1 and 2 shown below. Can these two structures be taken as the canonical forms of the resonance hybrid representing H3PO3? If not, give reasons for the same.
solution_text: The given structures cannot be taken as the canonical forms of the resonance hybrid of H 3 PO 3 because the positions of the atoms have changed. In structure (1) H is attached to P and while in structure (2) H is attached to O.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Although both CO 2 and H 2 O are triatomic molecules, the shape of H 2 O molecule is bent while that of CO 2 is linear. Explain this on the basis of dipole moment.
question_html: Although both CO2 and H2O are triatomic molecules, the shape of H2O molecule is bent while that of CO2  is linear. Explain this on the basis of dipole moment.
solution_text: In CO 2 there are two C=O bonds. Each C=O bond is a polar bond. The net dipole moment of CO 2 molecule is zero. This is possible only if CO 2 is a linear molecule (O=C=O). The dipole moments of two C=O bonds cancel the with each other. Whereas H 2 O molecule has a net dipole moment (1.84 D) H 2 O molecule has a bent structure because here the O-H bonds are oriented at an angle of 104.5° and do not cancel the bond moments of each other.
solution_html:
In CO2 there are two C=O bonds. Each C=O bond is a polar bond.
The net dipole moment of CO2 molecule is zero. This is possible only if CO2 is a linear molecule (O=C=O). The dipole moments of two C=O bonds cancel the with each other.
Whereas H2O molecule has a net dipole moment (1.84 D) H2O molecule has a bent structure because here the O-H bonds are oriented at an angle of 104.5° and do not cancel the bond moments of each other.

solution_text: In predicting the nature of the molecules: Molecules with specific dipole moments are polar in nature and those of zero dipole moments are non-polar in nature. In the determination of shapes of molecules. In calculating the percentage ionic character.
solution_html:
1. In predicting the nature of the molecules: Molecules with specific dipole moments are polar in nature and those of zero dipole moments are non-polar in nature.
  2. In the determination of shapes of molecules.
  3. In calculating the percentage ionic character.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define electro-negativity. How does it differ from electron gain enthalpy?
question_html: Define electro-negativity. How does it differ from electron gain enthalpy?
solution_text: Electro-negativity: Electro-negativity is the tendency of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons. It is the property of bonded atom. Whereas electron gain enthalpy is the tendency of an atom to attract outside electron. It is the property of an isolated atom.
solution_html:
Electro-negativity: Electro-negativity is the tendency of an atom to attract shared pair of electrons. It is the property of bonded atom.
Whereas electron gain enthalpy is the tendency of an atom to attract outside electron. It is the property of an isolated atom.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain with the help of suitable example polar covalent bond.
question_html: Explain with the help of suitable example polar covalent bond.
solution_text: When two atoms with different electro-negativity are linked to each other by a covalent bond, the shared pair of electrons will not in the center because of the difference in electro-negativity. For example, in hydrogen fluoride. molecule, fluorine has greater electronegativity than hydrogen. Thus the shared pair of electrons is displaced more towards fluorine atom, and acquire a partial negative charge ( {tex}\delta^-{/tex} ). At the same time, the hydrogen atom will have a partial positive charge ( {tex}\delta^+{/tex} ). Such a covalent bond is known as polar covalent bond or simply polar bond. lt is represented as: {tex}\delta^+{/tex} {tex}\delta^-{/tex} H - F (2,1) (4,0)
solution_html:
When two atoms with different electro-negativity are linked to each other by a covalent bond, the shared pair of electrons will not in the center because of the difference in electro-negativity. For example, in hydrogen fluoride.
molecule, fluorine has greater electronegativity than hydrogen. Thus the shared pair of electrons is displaced more towards fluorine atom, and acquire a partial negative charge ({tex}\delta^-{/tex}). At the same time, the hydrogen atom will have a partial positive charge ({tex}\delta^+{/tex}). Such a covalent bond is known as polar covalent bond or simply polar bond.
lt is represented as:
{tex}\delta^+{/tex} {tex}\delta^-{/tex}
H - F
(2,1) (4,0)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Arrange the bonds in order of increasing ionic character in the molecules; LiF, K 2 O, N 2 , SO 2 and ClF 3 .
question_html: Arrange the bonds in order of increasing ionic character in the molecules; LiF, K2O, N2, SO2 and ClF3.
solution_text: We know that, Ionic character {tex}\propto{/tex} lattice energy {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { size of ion }} \propto{/tex} change of ion. A non-polar molecule like N 2 has almost negligible ionic character. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of increasing ionic character is: {tex}N_2 < SO_2 <ClF_3 <K_2O <LiF.{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, Ionic character {tex}\propto{/tex} lattice energy {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { size of ion }} \propto{/tex} change of ion.
A non-polar molecule like N2 has almost negligible ionic character.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of increasing ionic character is:
{tex}N_2 < SO_2 <ClF_3 <K_2O <LiF.{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Apart from tetrahedral geometry, another possible geometry for CH 4 is square planar with the four H atoms at the corners of the square and the C atom at its center. Explain why CH 4 is not square planar?
question_html: Apart from tetrahedral geometry, another possible geometry for CH4 is square planar with the four H atoms at the corners of the square and the C atom at its center. Explain why CH4 is not square planar?
solution_text: Here consider the electronic configuration of carbon atom: 6 C: 1s 2 2s 2 2p 2 Whereas in the excited stage the orbital diagram can be, Therefore, the carbon atom undergoes sp 3 hybridization in CH 4 and takes the tetrahedral shape. But for a square planar shape, the hybridization of the central atom should be dsp 2 Since the atom of carbon does not have any d-orbitals to undergo hybridization, the structure of CH 4 cannot be square planar. Also, with a bond angle of 90° in square planar, the stability of CH 4 will be very less because of the repulsion between the bond pairs. Hence, the VSEPR theory also favors a tetrahedral structure for CH 4 .
solution_html:
Here consider the electronic configuration of carbon atom:
6C: 1s2 2s2 2p2

Whereas in the excited stage the orbital diagram can be,
Therefore, the carbon atom undergoes sp3 hybridization in CH4 and takes the tetrahedral shape.

But for a square planar shape, the hybridization of the central atom should be dsp2 Since the atom of carbon does not have any d-orbitals to undergo hybridization, the structure of CH4 cannot be square planar. Also, with a bond angle of 90° in square planar, the stability of CH4 will be very less because of the repulsion between the bond pairs. Hence, the VSEPR theory also favors a tetrahedral structure for CH4.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain why BeH 2 molecule has a zero dipole moment although the Be-H bonds are polar.
question_html: Explain why BeH2 molecule has a zero dipole moment although the Be-H bonds are polar.
solution_text: BeH 2 is a linear molecular (H-Be-H) the bond angle = 180°.Be-H bonds are polar due to the difference in their electronegativity but the bond polarities on both sides of Be cancel each other. Thus molecule has a resultant dipole moment zero.
solution_html: BeH2 is a linear molecular (H-Be-H) the bond angle = 180°.Be-H bonds are polar due to the difference in their electronegativity but the bond polarities on both sides of Be cancel each other. Thus molecule has a resultant dipole moment zero.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Which out of NH 3 and NF 3 has a higher dipole moment and why?
question_html: Which out of NH3 and NF3 has a higher dipole moment and why?
solution_text: In NH 3 and NF 3 the difference in electro-negativity is nearly same but the dipole moment of NH 3 (1.46 D) and NF 3 are (0.28 D). In NH 3 the dipole moments of the three N-H bonds are in the same direction to the lone pair of electron. But in NF 3 , the dipole moments of the three N-F bonds are is the direction opposite to that of the lone pair. Therefore, the resultant dipole moment in NH 3 is more than its NF 3 .
solution_html:
In NH3 and NF3 the difference in electro-negativity is nearly same but the dipole moment of NH3  (1.46 D) and  NF3  are (0.28 D).
In NH3 the dipole moments of the three N-H bonds are in the same direction to the lone pair of electron. But in NF3 , the dipole moments of the three N-F bonds are is the direction opposite to that of the lone pair. Therefore, the resultant dipole moment in NH3 is more than its NF3 .

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is meant by hybridisation of atomic orbitals? Describe the shapes of Sp, Sp 2 & Sp 3 hybrid orbitals.
question_html: What is meant by hybridisation of atomic orbitals? Describe the shapes of Sp, Sp2 & Sp3 hybrid orbitals.
solution_text: Hybridisation: It is defined as the process of intermixing of atomic orbitals of slightly different energies to give rise to newly hybridized orbitals having equivalent energy and identical shapes. Shapes of Orbitals: sp hybridisation: When one S and one p orbital intermix is called sp-hybridisation. For example, in BeF 2 . Be atom undergoes sp-hybridisation. It has a linear shape. Bond angle 180 o . Sp 2 hybridisation: One S and two p-orbitals get hybridized to form three equivalent hybrid orbitals. The three hybrid orbitals directed towards three corners of an equilateral triangle. It is, therefore, known as trigonal hybridisation. Sp 3 hybridization: One S and three p orbitals get hybridized to form four equivalent hybrid orbitals. These orbitals are directed towards the four corners of a regular tetrahedron.
solution_html:
Hybridisation: It is defined as the process of intermixing of atomic orbitals of slightly different energies to give rise to newly hybridized orbitals having equivalent energy and identical shapes.
Shapes of Orbitals:
sp hybridisation: When one S and one p orbital intermix is called sp-hybridisation. For example, in BeF2. Be atom undergoes sp-hybridisation. It has a linear shape. Bond angle 180o.
Sp2 hybridisation: One S and two p-orbitals get hybridized to form three equivalent hybrid orbitals. The three hybrid orbitals directed towards three corners of an equilateral triangle. It is, therefore, known as trigonal hybridisation.

Sp3 hybridization: One S and three p orbitals get hybridized to form four equivalent hybrid orbitals. These orbitals are directed towards the four corners of a regular tetrahedron.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Describe the change in hybridisation (if any) of the Al atom in the following reaction. {tex}\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
question_html:
Describe the change in hybridisation (if any) of the Al atom in the following reaction.
{tex}\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
solution_text: The valence orbital picture of aluminum in the ground state can be shown as: The orbital picture of aluminum in the excited state can be shown as: Hence, it undergoes sp 2 hybridization to give a trigonal planar arrangement (in AlCl 3 ). To form AlCl 4 – , the empty 3pz orbital also gets involved and the hybridization changes from sp 2 to sp 3 . As a result, the shape becomes tetrahedral.
solution_html:
The valence orbital picture of aluminum in the ground state can be shown as:

The orbital picture of aluminum in the excited state can be shown as:

Hence, it undergoes sp2 hybridization to give a trigonal planar arrangement (in AlCl3). To form AlCl4–, the empty 3pz orbital also gets involved and the hybridization changes from sp2 to sp3. As a result, the shape becomes tetrahedral.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Is there any change in the hybridisation of B and N atoms as a result of the reaction? BF 3 + NH 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F 3 B.NH 3
question_html:
Is there any change in the hybridisation of B and N atoms as a result of the reaction?
BF3 + NH3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F3B.NH3
solution_text: In BF 3 , B having 3 valence electrons, shares 1 valence electron with each F. So there are 3 bond pairs and 0 lone pair in BF 3 , so boron is sp 2 hybridised and in NH 3 , N having 5 valence electrons shares 1 valence electron with each H, so there are 3 bond pairs and 1 lone pair, so nitrogen is sp 3 hybridised. After the reaction, hybridisation of boron changes to sp 3 as now it forms 4 bonds. But hybridisation of nitrogen remains the same because N shares its lone pair with electron deficient B.
solution_html: In BF3, B having 3 valence electrons, shares 1 valence electron with each F. So there are 3 bond pairs and 0 lone pair in BF3, so boron is sp2 hybridised and in NH3, N having 5 valence electrons shares 1 valence electron with each H, so there are 3 bond pairs and 1 lone pair, so nitrogen is sp3 hybridised. After the reaction, hybridisation of boron changes to sp3 as now it forms 4 bonds. But hybridisation of nitrogen remains the same because N shares its lone pair with electron deficient B.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?
question_html: Considering x-axis as the internuclear axis which out of the following will not from a sigma bond and why?

options.explanation: For p-orbitals (l = 1), there are three possible orientations corresponding to m = -1, 0, +1 values. This means that there are three p - orbitals in each p-subshell. These are designated as p x , p y and p z ; These three orbitals are equal in energy but differ in their orientations. Each orbital consists of two lobes symmetrical about a particular axis. Depending upon the orientation of the lobes, these are designated as 2p x , 2p y and 2p z , as they are symmetrical about x, y and z-axes respectively. That is, 2p x orbital has two lobes symmetrical around x-axis and the lobes of 2p z orbital are symmetrical around z-axis while 2p y orbital has two lobes symmetrical around y-axix so it doesn't form sigma bond.

question_text: Which hybrid orbitals are used by carbon atoms in the following molecules? CH 3 –CH 3 ; CH 3 –CH = CH 2 ; CH 3 -CH 2 -OH; CH 3 -CHO CH 3 COOH

solution_text: According to structure, C 1 & C 2 are making 4 sigma bonds(single bond) each with the help of ones hybrid orbital & 3 p hybrid orbital, hence C 1 & C 2 are sp 3 hybridized Here C1 is making 4 sigma bond, therefore, its sp 3 hybridized, while C 2 and C 3 are making a double bond (1 sigma + 1 pie bond) therefore they both are s p 2 hybridized. Both the carbons C 1 & C 2 are making single bond(sigma bond), therefore they are s p 3 hybridized. From the structure it is clear that C1 is making sigma bonds only, therefore it is sp 3 hybridized. C 2 is making a double bond therefore it is sp 2 hybridized. C 1 is sp 3 hybridized and C 2 is s p 2 hybridized. Here C1 is making a sigma bond, therefore, it is in sp 3 hybridization state, while C 2 is making a double bond, it is in the sp 2 hybridized state.

  4. From the structure it is clear that C1 is making sigma bonds only, therefore it is sp3 hybridized. C2 is making a double bond therefore it is sp2 hybridized.
    C1 issp3hybridized and C2 issp2hybridized.



  5. Here C1 is making a sigma bond, therefore, it is in sp3 hybridization state, while C2 is making a double bond, it is in the sp2 hybridized state.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What do you understand by bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons? Illustrate by giving one example of each type.
question_html: What do you understand by bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons? Illustrate by giving one example of each type.
solution_text: The electron pair involved in sharing between two atoms during covalent bonding is called shared pair or bond pair. At the same time, the electron pair which is not involved in sharing is called a lone pair of electrons. For Example. In a case of CH 4 molecule.There are only 4 bond pairs. but in a case of H 2 O molecule.There are two bond pairs and two lone pairs.
solution_html:
The electron pair involved in sharing between two atoms during covalent bonding is called shared pair or bond pair. At the same time, the electron pair which is not involved in sharing is called a lone pair of electrons.
For Example. In a case of CH4 molecule.There are only 4 bond pairs.

but in a case of H2O molecule.There are two bond pairs and two lone pairs.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Distinguish between a sigma bond and a pi bond.
question_html: Distinguish between a sigma bond and a pi bond.
solution_text: Sigma bond ( {tex}\sigma{/tex} ) {tex}\sigma{/tex} -bond is formed by the axial overlap of the atomic orbitals. The bond is quite strong. Only one lobe of the p-orbitals is involved in the overlap. Electron cloud of the molecular orbital is symmetrical around the internuclear axis. pi bond ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) {tex}\pi{/tex} bond is formed by the sidewise overlap of atomic orbitals Comparatively weak bond. Both lobes of the p-orbitals are involved in the overlap. The electron cloud is not symmetrical.
solution_html:
Sigma bond ({tex}\sigma{/tex})

  1. {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is formed by the axial overlap of the atomic orbitals.
  2. The bond is quite strong.
  3. Only one lobe of the p-orbitals is involved in the overlap.
  4. Electron cloud of the molecular orbital is symmetrical around the internuclear axis.

pi bond ({tex}\pi{/tex})

  1. {tex}\pi{/tex}bond is formed by the sidewise overlap of atomic orbitals
  2. Comparatively weak bond.
  3. Both lobes of the p-orbitals are involved in the overlap.
  4. The electron cloud is not symmetrical.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Explain the formation of H 2 molecule on the basis of valence bond theory.
question_html: Explain the formation of H2 molecule on the basis of valence bond theory.
solution_text: Let us consider the combination between atoms of hydrogen H A and H B . If e A and e B be their respective electrons. As they tend to come closer, two different forces operate between the nucleus and the electron of the other and vice versa. The nuclei of the atom as well as their electrons repel each other. Energy is needed to overcome the force of repulsion. Although the magnitude of new attractive and repulsive forces is not same, the magnitude of the attractive forces is more. Thus when two hydrogen atoms approach each other the overall potential energy of the system decreases. Thus, a stable molecule of hydrogen is formed.
solution_html:
Let us consider the combination between atoms of hydrogen HA and HB. If eA and eB be their respective electrons.
As they tend to come closer, two different forces operate between the nucleus and the electron of the other and vice versa. The nuclei of the atom as well as their electrons repel each other. Energy is needed to overcome the force of repulsion. Although the magnitude of new attractive and repulsive forces is not same, the magnitude of the attractive forces is more. Thus when two hydrogen atoms approach each other the overall potential energy of the system decreases. Thus, a stable molecule of hydrogen is formed.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the important conditions required for the linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals.
question_html: Write the important conditions required for the linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals.
solution_text: (i) The combining atomic orbitals should have comparable energies. For example, 1s orbital of one atom can combine with a 1s atomic orbital of another atom, 2s can combine with 2s. (ii) The combining atomic orbitals must have proper orientations. So that they are able to overlap to a considerable extent. (iii) The extent of overlapping should be large.
solution_html:
(i) The combining atomic orbitals should have comparable energies. For example, 1s orbital of one atom can combine with a 1s atomic orbital of another atom, 2s can combine with 2s.
(ii) The combining atomic orbitals must have proper orientations. So that they are able to overlap to a considerable extent.
(iii) The extent of overlapping should be large.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Use molecular orbital theory to explain why the Be 2 molecule does not exist.
question_html: Use molecular orbital theory to explain why the Be2 molecule does not exist.
solution_text: E.C. of Be = 1s 2 2s 2 M.O.E.C. of Be 2 = {tex}\sigma^{2} 1 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{\star 2} 1 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{2} 2 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{\star 2} 2 \mathrm{s}{/tex} Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(4-4){/tex} = 0 Hence, Be 2 does not exist
solution_html:
E.C. of Be = 1s2 2s2
M.O.E.C. of Be2 = {tex}\sigma^{2} 1 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{\star 2} 1 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{2} 2 \mathrm{s} \sigma^{\star 2} 2 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(4-4){/tex}
= 0
Hence, Be2 does not exist

question_text: Compare the relative stability of the following species and indicate their magnetic properties; {tex}\text O_{2}, \text O_{2}^{+}, \text O_{2}^{-} {/tex} (superoxide), {tex}O_{2}^{2-}{/tex} (peroxide).
question_html: Compare the relative stability of the following species and indicate their magnetic properties; {tex}\text O_{2}, \text O_{2}^{+}, \text O_{2}^{-} {/tex} (superoxide), {tex}O_{2}^{2-}{/tex} (peroxide).
solution_text: O ​​​​​​2 + , O 2 and O 2 - are paramagnetic whereas O 2 2- are diamagnetic. The relative stability of the above species in decreasing order is {tex} \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}>\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the significance of plus and minus sign in representing the orbitals.
question_html: Write the significance of plus and minus sign in representing the orbitals.
solution_text: Plus and minus sign is used to identify the nature of electrons wave. Plus (+ve) sign denotes crest while (-ve) sign denotes trough.
solution_html: Plus and minus sign is used to identify the nature of electrons wave. Plus (+ve) sign denotes crest while (-ve) sign denotes trough.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Describe the hybridisation in case of PCl 5 . Why are the axial bonds longer as compared to equatorial bonds?
question_html: Describe the hybridisation in case of PCl5. Why are the axial bonds longer as compared to equatorial bonds?
solution_text: The ground state and excited state outer electronic configurations of phosphorus (Z = 15) are: Phosphorus atom is sp 3 d hybridized in the excited state (in PCl 5 the five sp 3 d orbitals overlap with the singly occupied p orbital of a chlorine atom to form 5 P-Cl sigma bond). These orbitals are filled by the electron pairs donated by five Cl atoms as PCl 5 . The five sp 3 d hybrid orbitals are directed towards the five corners of the trigonal bipyramidal. Hence, the geometry of PCl 5 can be represented as: There are five P–Cl sigma bonds in PCl 5 . Three P–Cl bonds lie in one plane and make an angle of 120° with each other. These bonds are called equatorial bonds. The remaining two P-Cl bonds lie above and below the equatorial plane and make an angle of 90° with the plane. These bonds are called axial bonds. As the axial bond pairs suffer more repulsion from the equatorial bond pairs, axial bonds are slightly longer than equatorial bonds.
solution_html:
The ground state and excited state outer electronic configurations of phosphorus (Z = 15) are:


Phosphorus atom is sp3d hybridized in the excited state (in PCl5 the five sp3d orbitals overlap with the singly occupied p orbital of a chlorine atom to form 5 P-Cl sigma bond). These orbitals are filled by the electron pairs donated by five Cl atoms as PCl5.

The five sp3d hybrid orbitals are directed towards the five corners of the trigonal bipyramidal. Hence, the geometry of PCl5 can be represented as:

There are five P–Cl sigma bonds in PCl5. Three P–Cl bonds lie in one plane and make an angle of 120° with each other. These bonds are called equatorial bonds. The remaining two P-Cl bonds lie above and below the equatorial plane and make an angle of 90° with the plane. These bonds are called axial bonds. As the axial bond pairs suffer more repulsion from the equatorial bond pairs, axial bonds are slightly longer than equatorial bonds.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Define hydrogen bonds. Is it weaker or stronger than the van der Waal's forces?
question_html: Define hydrogen bonds. Is it weaker or stronger than the van der Waal's forces?
solution_text: When hydrogen is attached to a highly electronegative element (F, O, N) in a covalent bonding the electrons of the covalent bond are shifted towards the more electronegative atom. Thus partially positively charged hydrogen atom forms a bond with the other more electronegative atom. This bond is known as a hydrogen bond. The hydrogen bond is stronger than the van der Waal's forces.
solution_html: When hydrogen is attached to a highly electronegative element (F, O, N) in a covalent bonding the electrons of the covalent bond are shifted towards the more electronegative atom. Thus partially positively charged hydrogen atom forms a bond with the other more electronegative atom. This bond is known as a hydrogen bond. The hydrogen bond is stronger than the van der Waal's forces.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is meant by the term bond order? Calculate the bond order of: {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+},{/tex} and {tex}\text{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} .
question_html: What is meant by the term bond order? Calculate the bond order of: {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+},{/tex} and {tex}\text{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex}.
solution_text: Bond order is defined as half of the difference between the number of electrons present in bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals. Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex} E.C. of {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^{1} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^{1} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^{1}{/tex} M.O. configuration of N 2 = {tex}[\sigma 1 s]^{2}[\sigma * 1 s]^{2}[\sigma 2 s]^{2}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex} Bond order (B.O) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex} {tex}= {1 \over 2}[10 - 4] = 3{/tex} B.O. of O 2 M.O. of configuration of O 2 = {tex}{(\sigma 1S)^2}{({\sigma ^ * }1S)^2}{(\sigma 2S)^2}{({\sigma ^ * }2S)^2}{(\pi 2{p_z})^2}{(\pi 2{p_x})^2}{(\pi 2{p_y})^2}{({\pi ^ * }2{p_x})^2}{/tex} B.O. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex} = {tex}= {1 \over 2}[10 - 6] = 2{/tex} M.O. of the configuration of {tex}O_{2}^{+}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{KK}[\sigma 2 s]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi * 2 p_{x}\right]^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [8-3] = 2.5 M.O. configuration of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{KK}[\sigma 2 \mathrm{s}]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}\right]^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [8-5] = 1.5
solution_html:
Bond order is defined as half of the difference between the number of electrons present in bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals.
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex}
E.C. of {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}=1 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^{1} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^{1} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^{1}{/tex}
M.O. configuration of N2= {tex}[\sigma 1 s]^{2}[\sigma * 1 s]^{2}[\sigma 2 s]^{2}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex}
Bond order (B.O) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex}
{tex}= {1 \over 2}[10 - 4] = 3{/tex}
B.O. of O2
M.O. of configuration of O2 = {tex}{(\sigma 1S)^2}{({\sigma ^ * }1S)^2}{(\sigma 2S)^2}{({\sigma ^ * }2S)^2}{(\pi 2{p_z})^2}{(\pi 2{p_x})^2}{(\pi 2{p_y})^2}{({\pi ^ * }2{p_x})^2}{/tex}
B.O. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}= {1 \over 2}[10 - 6] = 2{/tex}
M.O. of the configuration of {tex}O_{2}^{+}{/tex}= {tex}\mathrm{KK}[\sigma 2 s]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi * 2 p_{x}\right]^{1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[8-3] = 2.5
M.O. configuration of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{KK}[\sigma 2 \mathrm{s}]^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left[\sigma^{*} 2 s\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sigma 2 p_{z}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi 2 p_{y}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}\right]^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left[\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}\right]^{1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[8-5] = 1.5
